title: OS Command Injection
summary: A host-lookup tool runs a shell command with your input. Five stages filter it a little better each time — raw concatenation, a semicolon blacklist, an operator blacklist, quoted arguments, an unanchored allowlist — until the command runs with no shell at all.
goal: Make the host-lookup tool run a command of your choosing (e.g. id) — proven by output like uid=… that a hostname lookup would never produce.
goalSecure: Try the earlier payloads (; id, && id, $(id), quote breakout) — all should be treated as a literal hostname, while a real lookup still works.
rootCause: User input is placed into a string handed to a shell, so shell metacharacters are interpreted as syntax and run additional commands.
lessons: Building a shell command by concatenation lets metacharacters execute arbitrary commands.
Blacklisting one separator (;) ignores &&, ||, |, newlines, and more.
Command substitution ($()/backticks) executes with no chaining operator at all.
Wrapping a value in quotes inside a shell string isn't escaping — it can be closed.
An allowlist regex without ^…$ anchors validates 'contains a good char', not 'is all good chars'.
The fix is to not use a shell: pass arguments directly to the program (execFile/spawn).

stages.title: Raw shell concatenation
stages.defense: None — the host is dropped into a shell string.

stages.title: Semicolon blacklist
stages.defense: Rejects the host if it contains ";".

stages.title: Quoted argument
stages.defense: Wraps the host in double quotes; strips $ and backticks.

stages.title: Unanchored allowlist
stages.defense: Requires the host to match a hostname character class.

stages.title: No shell — argument vector
stages.defense: Passes the host as an argv element to execFile (no shell).
